slides.title: HTTP Message Signatures for automated traffic

polls.text: Do we think this is the right general direction / starting point?

chat.author: Gary Illyes
chat.text: Good to see you haven't given up on that!

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: Point of order: MOLE should be pronounced like the sauce, not the mammal

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Richard I assumed the Portuguese word for "soft".  Can you provide appropriate IPA as a guide?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: That would be [ˈmole]

chat.author: Michael P
chat.text: Might be too late, but the L in MOLE being unLinkable feels wrong (the acronym implies the opposite of what's being aimed for!)

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Especially as MOUE is also a word

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Can we sneak in an s and go for https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moules-frites ?

chat.author: Thom Vaughan
chat.text:
IPA:    /ˈmo.le/
XSAMPA:    /“mo.le/
for extra portability

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I would say formally nothing, but actually it may say something, like if it's in my GitHub repo.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Pseudonyms derived from certs could be interesting as an option for the opaque mode.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Isn't the key_id a pseudonym?

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Yes, it isn't like we need to invent pseudonyms here, but binding it to a property of the domain becomes possible again.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: A URI with no defined semantics is quite interesting for this.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: it seems like not many of the use cases depended on domain binding

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @MT are you thinking something like a UUID URI?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The nice thing about a random URI is that you don't drag in the reputation of the domain.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Ted Hardie an HTTPS resource.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Ted Hardie an HTTPS resource.

there are other types of URI?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: A UUID URI doesn't give you anything like the ability to tie two keys together, because you need to have a resource that can change state.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Well, so the way this works in the draft you can supply either (1) a URI or (2) a domain name

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: In the former case, there are no formal semantics, but there may be actual semantics

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Using the key id for continuity is an error.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: like if the URI is https://github.com/ekr/myapp/foo.jso

chat.author: Dick Hardt
chat.text: A host name can also effectively random

chat.author: Dick Hardt
chat.text: gagsjdj.examole.com

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Dick Hardt a random domain name comes with overheads and costs that don't make sense to me.  If you are going to bind to a domain name, you do that because you want to drag in the reputation of the domain.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Whereas if I give it an origin like "example.com", because it's in well-known, that means that the origin is endorsing it

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Actually, what might be interesting is having a random URI that you can use to enforce the order in which an entity flows through the website, i.e. enforcing that you looked at robots.txt before you can access anything else.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Simply selecting an HTTP URI carries some semantics, though I agree a specific resources may not give you a lot more.  So you may want to describe this in  some way other than "no defined semantics".

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: In the case of <random string>.<domain>, that will, at least to some degree, draw on the reputation of <domain>

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: just use DIDs

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Ted Hardie said:

Simply selecting an HTTP URI carries some semantics, though I agree a specific resources may not give you a lot more.  So you may want to describe this in  some way other than "no defined semantics".

Fair enough. Maybe "no endorsement"

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I think that this "endorsement" concept is clearly the best way to handle this.  That removes the need for the "I am Spartacus" defenses in the current draft.

chat.author: Dick Hardt
chat.text: @martin what overheads? It’s pretty simple these days — you have a dns record — but you don’t need to drag in the domain for reputation

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Dick Hardt To put it differently -- if what you ultimately want is an HTTPS URL you can fetch from, why not just use an HTTPS URL

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I really don't understand the split Thibault is making here.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
Dick Hardt
chat.text: random.example.com is reputation ally not any different than example.com/random as for as the domain is concerned

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Maybe...

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: https://github.com/martinthomson is reputationally different from github.com

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: PSL-like

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: But "martinthomson.github.io" isn't that far off what you want.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: <random>.<domain> is like <domain>/<random> only to the extent that the delegation rules regarding each are the same.  They do reduce to the same thing, but <random>.<domain> is more annoying to arrange.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I dont know how I feel about a "put it in the DNS" ID. If the domain is wrongly suspended would it still work?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: This was intended to be a helpful amendment, btw.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I thought it was helpful.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: Dequeuing because I was basically just going to +1 EKR and Justin

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The question that follows is whether you need to have a way to have no URL.  I think that this is natural, but it means that you need a process for generating an identifier that is a not a URL.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: +1 to providing keys by value

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The same goes for URLs that end up being unreachable.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: And point out that if you make the tweak to the opaque mechanism to use the URL as the identifier, you get the domain form for free just by recognizing "hey this is a .well-known URL"

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: (Whether that's a good idea I'm less certain about)

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: The way I would think of this is that the key is at some URI and the semantics are "This URI endorses this key".  If the URI has semantics, then you can infer something about the entity which endorses the key. So, for instance, if the URI were https;//example.com/.well-known/keys.json you could still make that inference. From that perspective, the version where you supply just the domain and the server constructs /.well-known is just syntactic sugar

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: There is a minor issue with URLs that cannot be resolved with that design, Richard.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @MT there are lots of URIs that are easy to mint at the moment, is there an issue with re-using one of those, when you simply want to generate an identifier?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: What if three different entities say that their URI is "some.url/that/does/not/exist"?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

What if three different entities say that their URI is "some.url/that/does/not/exist"?

Say more?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: I would think that if the URL doesn't resolve, the signature doesn't verify, end of story

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The problem is the case where the URL does not endorse the key.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: But Richard's answer to that works.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Or you could fall back to a synthetic identifier derived from the public key.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: i mean, if the URL doesn't resolve, you don't have a key to verify with

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Oh, I thought that the plan was to put the key in a header (or that was Dick's idea and Thibault is a co-author).

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I thought Thibault said he was tkaing that out!

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: If that is the case, that simplifies things.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Could we do key rotation for pseudonymous keys by signing the new key with the old key? It's only a forward binding, but that might be enough.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: What Richard said here is what I meant when I was saying "I reserve the right to be against this on principle"

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I don't think they're separate, really

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: what do we mean by "identifying" the bots? like we verify their real life identity? or is it this some "technical" identifier?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text:
bc my answer will be "no" or maybe "don't know" until we see the how the anonym
ous-mode one works

chat.author: Shay Chin
chat.text: Wouldn’t that create an issue if the rotation gets missed the auth would be out of sync?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Farzaneh said:

what do we mean by "identifying" the bots? like we verify their real life identity? or is it this some "technical" identifier?

This does both

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Richard Barnes any particular objection or just crypto heebie jeebies as someone might say?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

Farzaneh said:

what do we mean by "identifying" the bots? like we verify their real life identity? or is it this some "technical" identifier?

This does both

Where the identifier is "domain name"

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: then we will make domain name registration harder and identity based no?

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I agree with Richard. but we are in the rough ...

chat.author: Shay Chin
chat.text: What about having multiple sigs during a key rotation

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Farzaneh said:

I agree with Richard. but we are in the rough ...

As in diamonds or as in golf?

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I just think if you have anonymous and identified bots in the end you will have a discriminatory sysmtem

chat.author: Brent Zundel
chat.text: That's my worry exactly

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Farzaneh said:

I just think if you have anonymous and identified bots in the end you will have a discriminatory sysmtem

Well you know where to find the mic

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: :100: to doubting voluntariness

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Eric Trouton ++

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Ostensibly optional

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: @Jonthan ha!! I want to think as in shining diamonds!

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: it will be optional in the sense that it is optional to use the web

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: It's 3.45 AM here so I dont know if you want to hear me this early.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: am I wrong that tying this to domains might impact domain registration?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: it seems unlikely to make domain registration more of a dumpster fire than it already is

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: so can the registrar says yo you are doing scraping wrong I suspend you.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I mean having a small speed bump in that a domain costs some amount of money makes this mechanism more likely to be workable in practice

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Farzaneh said:

so can the registrar says yo you are doing scraping wrong I suspend you.

That would be assuming that registrars do some amount of sensible moderation.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: well it's more or less  accessible dumpster fire. I wonder if the regulators ask people who want to register domains to hand in their ID (oh some do that already ...)

chat.author: Pablo González
chat.text: Can we mute the in person room WHile EKR presents? it is producing echo

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yes please mute the room

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: ekr echo

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: eko

chat.author: David Schinazi
chat.text: Is this better?

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: solved, thx

chat.author: David Schinazi
chat.text: Great

chat.author: Pablo González
chat.text: thx

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: this presentation brought to you by Dun & Bradstreet™

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Can Alice or Bob offer a presentation that exploits MoLE's anchor indistinguishability?  As in (D&B OR GS1 OR ...)?

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: You can also provide more budget.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: so these anchors can function like domain registrars? wait I just confused myself so the moderator is the attestor? who can be a moderator?

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: How does the moderator deal with concurrent access?

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: The moderator likely issues a pool of tokens per anchor token in practice.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Concurrent access can be handled by giving out multiple of these credentials.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The supply would need to be very limited, of course.

chat.author: Max Gendler
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

The supply would need to be very limited, of course.

Could you say more about this? It's not intuitive to me.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: For instance, per bot: Anchors would issue N Endorsements per epoch, each of which would create M issuances per Moderator, where each of those issuances would have C credits. You can make N, M, C small.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: oh ok so it's about client "technical" behavior ... not about "who" they are

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Let's say that you have a "quantity" associated with the credential of 100 as a unitary thing.  You could instead issue two credentials with 50 each.  This allows for two concurrent interaction chains.  But it also means that the overall usefulness of each is diminished AND killing one for bad behaviour doesn't hit the other.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: It is essentially an ephemeral, unjoinable database row corresponding to that bot, which is held by that bot and only properties about that row and its updates are proven to the Moderator.

chat.author: Max Gendler
chat.text: I think I'm tracking what you're saying, but am unsure. May bug you later after rereading. Thanks both

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: It is not sticky to that bot in the long term, they can simply throw it out and get a new one. But a bot that is conveying good behavior might be able to get long term access from a single Moderator credential.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: As Sam says, split it N ways and you get N-way concurrency.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: I can't work out how that could make sense, practically @Richard Barnes.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I like this. it solves a legitimate use case without asking people to hand in their ID.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I mean, the point of web-bot-auth is that it's for bots not people. Bots don't have an expectation of privacy.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: I think pseudonyms are indeed another valuable solution but doesn't capture the fully privacy-preserving use case, as you need a reason to accept a particular presentation and scarcity is that reason.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: but I am still trying to understand it. which takes a long time...

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: Jonathan bots that work on behalf of people need privacy and we have explained why.

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: @Martin Thomson you would essentially locate the privacy function in the anchor URL, e.g., by letting each bot have 1000 keys that it rotates through to obscure it's browsing habits

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Imagine you had an anchor at some URL that you could query with an opaque key that the bot provides.  You'd need something where the site that queries that URL can use it as an oracle based on an unlinkable, pseudonymous identifier that the bot provides...

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: But i'm just inventing this on the fly, so it may be totaly bogus

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Some bots work on behalf of people, but many (perhaps even most) bots work on behalf of companies too.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text:
Bots don't have an expectation of privacy.

Why is this? From my perspective, many bots have legitimate use cases which are not desired by the site, for which they need to evade identification.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: The 1000 keys option doesn't achieve the same properties as the MoLE design

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Consumer protection is an example that has been repeatedly brought up.

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: +1 Pablo on the org who  can be a trusted anchor for bots/agents when accessing sites. It's an interesting problem of who to trust and why

chat.author: Jorge Silva
chat.text:
+1 
Adding to Pablo's question, aren't we going to have an issue with more malicious Anchors?

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: "Some bots work on behalf of people, but many (perhaps even most) bots work on behalf of companies too." yeah and that led to the unfortunate bot identification approach ...

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: So bots that need privacy / secrecy shouldn't use this scheme. I may have a right not to provide my identity, but I definitely don't have a right to use someone else's identity

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: +1 Samuel

chat.author: Chris Needham
chat.text: What in this system gives sites the confidence to trust the anchors?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text:
Chris Needham said:

What in this system gives sites the confidence to trust the anchors?

Nothing in the proposal.  Presumably some program similar to the PKI or other ways that relying parties choose who to trust.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Establishing Anchor reputation is a key bootstrapping part, which involves Anchors filtering the bots they endorse.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: also this group has AI agents that work on behlaf of people in scope... I might be wrong but it can affect access to content and services if we use identification. you are the agent from so and so region and wearing a red shirt? BLOCK.

chat.author: Alissa Cooper
chat.text: Existence of both mechanisms does seem like it will disicentivize the use of this one. Why would sites trust these anchors if they are mostly endorsing bots that the sites would rather block or just don't care about allowing?

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text:
+1 Chris, that's my question as well.
I like this proposal for the anonymous approach (which I support, just as the proposal of Thibault, I'm one of those that is clear on: they both serve a purpose)

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yeah Alissa. +10000

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Alissa Cooper that is the essential problem that this sort of design reveals.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: But I didnt win that argument... they want to identify bots

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: They'd use different Anchors and Moderators, if the site doesn't want bots.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: MoLE isn't a global deployment, it is an architecture.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Daniel Gillmor the identity of the anchor will determine what the semantics are.

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: +1 Justin on kicking up the trust one level. Some sites will require to register with their chosen Anchor and  can work with those Anchors to enforce certain identity verification and trust verification from the Anchor they build trust with.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: who is potentially this anchor. can you name a company that could potentially be one? like CDNs?

chat.author: Max Gendler
chat.text:
Alissa Cooper said:

Existence of both mechanisms does seem like it will disicentivize the use of this one. Why would sites trust these anchors if they are mostly endorsing bots that the sites would rather block or just don't care about allowing?

IMHO - this problem exists whether or not the first solution exists. Trusting anchors will be a similar issue regardless of the other solution existing - anchor's trust will be a factor of who anchors are endorsing, no?

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: +1 Max

chat.author: Alissa Cooper
chat.text: There is an independent anchor trust challenge, but my point is that if the big bots use the identity-based mechanism and don't bother with the complexity of this mechanism, then sites have no reason to support this mechanism at all.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: This can, if used in the way @Jonathan Hoyland describes, reduce to the other design.  Just much more complicated.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: not who, Max. based on technical behavior. not who you are and where you are.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Alissa, i think that is a real concern

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yeah the incentive structure here ...

chat.author: Max Gendler
chat.text:
Alissa Cooper said:

There is an independent anchor trust challenge, but my point is that if the big bots use the identity-based mechanism and don't bother with the complexity of this mechanism, then sites have no reason to support this mechanism at all.

Understood, but I think Jonathan's question points to what I was saying. Anchors can be created that choose to work only for 'big-bots' so whether the other system exists or not, the problem exists

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

This can, if used in the way Jonathan Hoyland describes, reduce to the other design.  Just much more complicated.

OK so a goal is to make sure that's not how things get deployed.

chat.author: Kevin Kelley
chat.text: Alissa--don't big bots already use identity-based mechanisms in the sense that they publish IP ranges and/or rDNS records?  It's already difficult to convincingly impersonate a large-scale bot's product token.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Kevin Kelley said:

Alissa--don't big bots already use identity-based mechanisms in the sense that they publish IP ranges and/or rDNS records?  It's already difficult to convincingly impersonate a large-scale bot's product token.

I think one of the original issues was that some of the big bots weren't able to keep their lists of IP addresses up-to-date.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Some bots are very easy to impersonate at scale.

chat.author: Alissa Cooper
chat.text: Sure, big bots use various identity-based things already that are somewhat broken in different ways, I was wondering, if sites are deciding about whether to support one or more new mechanisms because of that brokenness, what will be their incentive to support this one.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Yes, that's possible, but the reputation is problematic.  If the site wants to do that you have characteristic X if you have presented an endorsement that indicates eiether (X or Y or Z) it's not clear that satisifies their need.

chat.author: Pablo González
chat.text: Wuuldn't this be served by an umbrella org signing WBA on behalf of a group of entities instead of needing MoLE?

chat.author: Richard Barnes
chat.text: The ability to reduce to Thibaut's design seems like a feature

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: i dunno, if it's going to reduce to Thibaut's design, it is a lot of additional heavyweight organizational and technical complexity.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text:
these are in scope: - Crawlers for AI training

AI agents retrieving or interacting with content on behalf of end users

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Alissa Cooper said:

Sure, big bots use various identity-based things already that are somewhat broken in different ways, I was wondering, if sites are deciding about whether to support one or more new mechanisms because of that brokenness, what will be their incentive to support this one.

That is the biggest question to my mind.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: You could also imagine a government deciding that they needed to be an anchor for sovereignty reasons, and that goes all sorts of bad directions.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: @Jonathan very much so.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: with the identity approach, sites might just allow big bots and they can pay-per-crawl them on top of that and then we will be hanging out on a non-Internet

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Farzaneh We certainly wouldn't have _the_ Internet any more, we'd have a few internets.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yes Jonathan!!!

chat.author: Kevin Kelley
chat.text: "A distributed feedback mechanism so that anchors know whether to continue endorsing a client" seems like it's at odds with what we heard from Thibault in the MoLE session, which is that the anchors are only about bootstrapping.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: about the governments being anchors...

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Privacy preserving feedback to the anchors seems like it is going to be difficult, in part because it would be easy to game a positive reputation by creating potemkin sites to provide positive feedback.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: it's sybils all the way down

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: @Ted then we need a separate reputation system for the sites that provide feedback...

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: That's the centralizing risk we discussed a bit earlier. A powerful anchor or set of anchors becomes a centralizing element of this ecosystem.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: Maybe the anchor can can/should provide some additional bit(s) of information (like human or bot or even what kind of bot in a behavioural sense) that is not identifying and you only provide feedback if the behaviour of the bot set doesn't align with the described properties

chat.author: Micke Nordin
chat.text: Seems to me the tthe identifying approach will be much less reliant on central infrastructures and will be more friendly to small bots. The non-authenticating approach seems to do the opposite of what it advertises, and give a lot of weight to large centralized infrastructures and big players

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: I think the problem how we provide additional information about expected bahvior is the same in both cases

chat.author: Pablo González
chat.text: +1 to Micke

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: +1 Micke

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yeah the anchor can say the endorsement is based on rate limiting etc etc. but how the anchor finds that out...

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: which identifying approach, Micke?

chat.author: Micke Nordin
chat.text: I mean, serving a .well-known/jwks.json and signing requests is very easy, compared to try to get your bot into the same anchor where google-bot is.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: yeah I totally agree. when we go into territories of privacy preserving we kind of don't have anonymity anymore.

chat.author: Chris Needham
chat.text: Fully agree with what's being said right now

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: What incentive does a big bot operator have to sign up to a generic anchor, and further, what incentive does a website operator have to listen to a generic anchor if all the big operators have custom anchors?

chat.author: Micke Nordin
chat.text: Yes, it does not seem to be a difference between identifying or non-identifying, but rather centralized vs. distrubuted approach.

chat.author: Alissa Cooper
chat.text: this approach could be quite distributed if there were lots of anchors

chat.author: Chris Needham
chat.text: I'm not convinced the assumption that sites want to block everything other than the big operators is a valid one

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: why not Chris? especially if those are the ones that give them money per crawl?

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: +1 Marwan

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I think we have forgotten why we came up with this "anonymous approach"

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Most people want to spend as little time as possible moderating / configuring their site. Selecting a few big bots probably solves 99% of your use cases in under a minute.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: we would also have less of a problem here if crawling would be more efficient... just saying...

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: +1 to the Anchors creating the positive floor.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: (and not everybody would do their own crawling to do exactly the same thing)

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: @Mirja: i blame "web 2.0"

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: I feel having a way to do this for the identified variant which is not domains would also be ideal, pseudonyms rooted in a positive floor.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: That is what I find missing from the identified variant.

chat.author: Mark Nottingham
chat.text: A system that addresses the problem that Ted points out would be quite valuable.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: oh Ted reminded us why we came up with this... (just gotta say well behaved bot in a technical sense. reputation based on technical parameters. not the bot nationality.)

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: If you could bootstrap trust in a stateful credential into a positive trust level without being endorsed by anyone, it would be ideal. I don't know how to do it effectively. One naive example would be a pseudonym derived from a zero-knowledge proof of an expensive-ish proof of work, but that has issues.

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: My worry - this takes a very hard technical problem and transforms it into a very hard policy problem

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: NGL I can see the market for aged identities now.

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I think the endorsement can be about following the best practices for data collection. for example, practices like this https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-farzdusa-webbot-datacollection/

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: Jonathan: by "now" are you referring to the existing market? because it exists

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: The multi-stage is for openness, it is not a technical requirement of the reputational, stateful credentials.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: It does indeed, but not for MoLE (as far as I know :p)

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Is it mole@ietf or still pact?

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: mole

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: unfortunate market of age verification you mean?

chat.author: Kevin Kelley
chat.text: Hi, Gary!

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: is that real Gary? show your ID to create trust!

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Samuel Schlesinger said:

The multi-stage is for openness, it is not a technical requirement of the reputational, stateful credentials.

Sam, I just noticed this. Maybe you and I can sync up on this at some point, because it's possible I've misunderstood some technical questions.

chat.author: Kevin Kelley
chat.text: Farz: he has an endorsement from the same anchor that GoogleBot does ;)

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Happy to sync and chat.

chat.author: Mirja Kühlewind
chat.text: at least he behaves like Gary usually does

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: ahahah Right Kevin!!

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: you are trying to codify it?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: We need more Gary at the IETF!  Why is he trapped in Mordor/Google?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: (I mean, it's not like Zurich is that far...)

chat.author: Kevin Kelley
chat.text: Memes aside, I'm very supportive of the idea of a set of crawler best practices.

chat.author: Samuel Schlesinger
chat.text: Mandatory compliance trainings for bots.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I mean there are already crawler best practices defined in other venues

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text:
I think everyone is in favor of having crawler best practices :)
All agents should go through those compliance training, Samuel!

chat.author: Gary Illyes
chat.text: @justin umm...

chat.author: Gary Illyes
chat.text:
We need more Gary at the IETF! Why is he trapped in Mordor/Google? (I mean, it's not like Zurich is that far...)

yes

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: sorry is this the crawler practices you wrote with Mirja, Gary?

chat.author: Gary Illyes
chat.text: yes

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I have expressed my concerns

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Farzaneh said:

I have expressed my concerns

Can you share a link?

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: we also provided this as https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-farzdusa-webbot-datacollection/ should we submit as best practices?

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: I said it on the mailing list Jonathan will put it on an accessible blog!!

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: WIMSE practices doc: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-wimse-workload-identity-practices-05

chat.author: Elaine Ly
chat.text: Could you explain more about how the new bot behavior you've observed is different from old bot behavior?

chat.author: Gary Illyes
chat.text: eg. not respecting 429, no caching

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: "Crawlers must support and respect the Robots Exclusion Protocol." so thats one of the practices. which is very controversial because there are good reasons not to follow robots exclusion protocol

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I mean that just suggests (to me) that the BCP would have a SHOULD

chat.author: Philippe Dellaert
chat.text: Thanks Chairs, and all presenters and everyone for the discussions. Always fun times!

chat.author: Farzaneh
chat.text: thanks
